Call (Call Us)

24/7 Emergency Storm Damage Tree Removal

Serving Cartersville, GA and the surrounding area.

Call (Call Us) Get a Free Estimate

Georgia storm season doesn't wait for business hours. Spring and summer bring fast-moving thunderstorms with straight-line winds that flatten pines in minutes, and every few years a tropical system remnant tracks up from the Gulf and dumps enough rain to turn Bartow County's red clay to soup -- which is exactly when root systems let go and trees start coming down.

We run 24/7 emergency response for storm damage across Cartersville, Acworth, Emerson, White, Kingston, and Euharlee. If a tree is down on your house, car, fence, or blocking your driveway, we treat that as urgent and move it to the front of the schedule -- day, night, or holiday.

The most common calls after a storm: a pine leaning on a roofline but not fully down, a limb through a window or gutter, a tree across the only driveway out of a property, or a tree resting on a power line (which we won't touch until the utility company confirms the line is de-energized -- your safety and ours comes first, every time).

Emergency work costs more than scheduled work -- expect a premium above standard rates for after-hours or same-day storm calls, since it means pulling a crew off another job or out after dark with lights and extra safety gear. But we don't pad it -- you're paying for urgency and risk, not markup for the sake of it. We'll tell you the number before we start, not after.

If a tree is on your house, our first priority is stabilizing the situation -- getting weight off the structure safely -- before full removal and cleanup. We work directly with insurance adjusters when a tree has caused property damage and can provide photos, cut logs, and documentation for your claim.

During active severe weather, wait until the storm passes before calling -- we can't safely dispatch a crew into lightning or high wind. The moment it's safe to move, call and we'll get a crew routed to you. In a widespread storm event hitting multiple towns at once, we triage by hazard level: trees on structures and blocked emergency access first, yard debris and non-blocking damage after.

Georgia's storm season runs roughly March through November, with the sharpest spike in April-May straight-line wind events and August-September tropical remnants. Bartow County rarely gets hit as hard as coastal Georgia, but a single strong cell moving through Cartersville or Acworth can down a dozen trees in twenty minutes -- we keep extra crew capacity on call during those months for exactly that reason.

Common Questions

How fast can you respond to storm damage?

For trees on a house, blocking a driveway, or resting on a power line, we treat it as urgent and dispatch as soon as it's safe to move after the storm passes. Response time depends on how many calls are coming in during a widespread event, but house-on-tree and blocked-access calls always go first.

What if the tree is touching a power line?

We won't cut a tree that's in contact with a power line until the utility confirms it's de-energized. That's not us being slow -- it's the line between a bad day and a fatal one. Call the utility company first if lines are down or a tree is resting on them, then call us for the tree work once it's cleared safe.

Do you charge extra for emergency calls?

Yes, after-hours and same-day emergency response carries a premium over scheduled daytime work -- typically higher than our standard removal pricing depending on time of day and job complexity. We quote the number before starting, and it reflects the real cost of an emergency dispatch, not inflated pricing during a crisis.

Can you help with an insurance claim for storm damage?

Yes. We document the damage with photos, provide an itemized invoice, and can speak with your adjuster about the scope of work if needed. Keep your own photos of the damage before we start clearing, since adjusters usually want to see the original condition.
